questions.question:
Which of the following is correct?
A. B trees are for storing data on disk and B+ trees are for main memory.
B. Range queries are faster on B+ trees.
C. B trees are for primary indexes and B+ trees are for secondary indexes.
D. The height of a B+ tree is independent of the number of records.
questions.answer: B. Range queries are faster on B+ trees.
questions.to_find: Identify the correct statement about B trees and B+ trees

understand.plain: B+ trees store all data pointers in leaf nodes, and leaf nodes are linked as a list enabling sequential range scans. B trees store data in internal nodes too, so range queries require traversal up and down the tree and are slower.

keywords.explain: A variant of B tree where all data pointers are in leaf nodes; internal nodes hold only routing keys. Leaf nodes are linked for sequential access.
keywords.example: Searching for records with keys between 10 and 20 requires one root-to-leaf traversal then linear scan of linked leaves.

keywords.explain: A query that retrieves all records whose key falls within a specified interval [lo, hi].
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM employees WHERE salary BETWEEN 50000 AND 80000.

keywords.explain: A self-balancing tree where data records reside in both internal and leaf nodes; no linked leaf list, so range queries are costlier.
keywords.example: Finding all keys in [5, 15] may require multiple subtree visits since matching keys appear at any level.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='13'><rect width='420' height='150' fill='var(--color-bg,#fff)'/><text x='10' y='20' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>B+ Tree leaf layer linked list</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='80' height='30' rx='4' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='50' y='55' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>5 | 8</text><rect x='110' y='35' width='80' height='30' rx='4' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='150' y='55' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>12 | 15</text><rect x='210' y='35' width='80' height='30' rx='4' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='250' y='55' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>18 | 22</text><rect x='310' y='35' width='80' height='30' rx='4' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='350' y='55' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>25 | 30</text><line x1='90' y1='50' x2='110' y2='50' stroke='#3B82F6' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='190' y1='50' x2='210' y2='50' stroke='#3B82F6' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='290' y1='50' x2='310' y2='50' stroke='#3B82F6' stroke-width='2'/><text x='10' y='100' fill='#555'>Range [12,22]: find 12 in leaf, scan right to 15, 18, 22 sequentially.</text><text x='10' y='120' fill='#555'>B tree: data in internal nodes requires tree traversal for range scan.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: B+ tree leaf nodes linked left-to-right enable sequential range scanning, unlike B trees where data at internal nodes disrupts sequential access.

given.aim: Determine which statement about B trees vs B+ trees is correct

terms.meaning: In B+ trees, leaf nodes are connected as a doubly linked list, enabling linear scan for range queries.
terms.example: After reaching leaf containing key 10, the next leaf is visited directly via the next pointer.
terms.connects: This is what makes range queries faster on B+ trees.

terms.meaning: B trees store actual data records in internal nodes, while B+ trees store only routing keys in internal nodes.
terms.example: In a B tree, finding key 15 might end at an internal node; in B+ tree, you always reach the leaf.
terms.connects: This structural difference explains why range queries differ in performance.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against known B tree and B+ tree properties, identify which is universally true.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.apply: B trees and B+ trees are both used for disk-based storage. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: Neither structure is exclusively for main memory.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.apply: B+ trees link leaf nodes. A range query [lo,hi] searches for lo then scans linked leaves to hi. Much faster than B tree range scan. Option B is TRUE.
steps.note: Linked leaf nodes are the key advantage of B+ trees for range queries.

steps.title: Evaluate options C and D
steps.apply: C: Both tree types can serve any index type. FALSE. D: Height depends on number of records. FALSE.
steps.note: Height h satisfies n >= 2*ceil(m/2)^(h-1)-1, so height grows with n.

solution.result: B. Range queries are faster on B+ trees.

questions.formula_note: B+ tree linked leaf list enables O(range_size + log n) range queries versus potentially O(range_size * log n) traversal in B trees.

questions.question: Consider a B+ tree in which the maximum number of keys in a node is 5. What is the minimum number of keys in any non-root node?
questions.to_find: Minimum number of keys in a non-root node of a B+ tree with maximum 5 keys per node

understand.plain: A B+ tree of order m means each node holds at most m pointers (and m-1 keys). Non-root nodes must be at least half full to keep the tree balanced. With maximum 5 keys, order m = 6. The minimum keys for a non-root node is ceil(m/2) - 1 = ceil(6/2) - 1 = 2.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of pointers (children) in an internal node. A node with m pointers has at most m-1 keys.
keywords.example: Max 5 keys means max 6 pointers, so order m = 6.

keywords.explain: Non-root nodes must hold at least ceil(m/2) - 1 keys to maintain balance.
keywords.example: For m=6: ceil(6/2) - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2 minimum keys in any non-root node.

keywords.explain: B+ tree nodes must be at least half full (except root) to guarantee O(log n) performance.
keywords.example: If max keys = 5, then min keys = 2, so a node can have 2, 3, 4, or 5 keys.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 340 130' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='13'><rect width='340' height='130' fill='var(--color-bg,#fff)'/><text x='10' y='20' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>B+ tree order m=6 max keys=5</text><rect x='10' y='35' width='160' height='28' rx='4' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='90' y='53' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>Max: k1 | k2 | k3 | k4 | k5</text><rect x='10' y='80' width='130' height='28' rx='4' fill='#DCFCE7' stroke='#22C55E'/><text x='75' y='98' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>Min non-root: k1 | k2</text><text x='185' y='55' fill='#666'>5 keys max</text><text x='155' y='100' fill='#666'>ceil(6/2)-1=2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: For max 5 keys (order 6), minimum non-root keys = ceil(6/2) - 1 = 2.

given.aim: Compute the minimum number of keys in any non-root node when max keys per node is 5

terms.meaning: The upper bound on keys in a single node, determining the order of the B+ tree.
terms.connects: Order m drives the minimum occupancy formula.

terms.meaning: The formula for minimum keys in a non-root B+ tree node; ensures nodes are at least half full.

given.plan: Identify order m from max keys (m = 6), then apply minimum keys formula ceil(m/2) - 1.

steps.title: Determine order m
steps.apply: max_keys = 5, so m = 5 + 1 = 6. Each internal node has at most 6 pointers and 5 keys.
steps.note: Order m refers to the maximum number of pointers, not keys.

steps.title: Apply minimum keys formula
steps.note: This applies to non-root nodes; the root can have as few as 1 key.

questions.formula_note: Non-root B+ tree node minimum keys = ceil(m/2) - 1 where m is the order (max pointers per node); ensures at least 50% node utilization.

questions.question:
Consider a B+ tree in which the search key is 12 bytes long, block size is 1024 bytes, record pointer is 10 bytes, and block pointer is 8 bytes. The maximum order (number of pointers) of the B+ tree index node is:
A. 50
B. 51
C. 52
D. 53
questions.to_find: Maximum order (number of pointers) of a B+ tree index node given block size, key size, and pointer sizes

understand.plain: An internal B+ tree node with m pointers contains m-1 search keys and m block pointers. All content must fit in one disk block: m * block_ptr_size + (m-1) * key_size <= block_size. Solving gives the maximum m.

keywords.explain: An internal node with m pointers: [P1|K1|P2|K2|...|K(m-1)|Pm], using m block pointers and (m-1) search keys.
Total bytes used by pointers and keys must fit in one block: m * ptr_size + (m-1) * key_size <= block_size.
Block pointer (8 bytes) points to a child block in the B+ tree. Record pointer (10 bytes) points to an actual data record, used only in leaf nodes.
keywords.example: Internal nodes use block pointers (8B); leaf nodes use record pointers (10B).

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 110' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect width='400' height='110' fill='var(--color-bg,#fff)'/><text x='10' y='18' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>Internal node layout m=3 shown</text><rect x='10' y='30' width='30' height='28' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='25' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>P1</text><rect x='40' y='30' width='50' height='28' fill='#FEF3C7' stroke='#F59E0B'/><text x='65' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>K1</text><rect x='90' y='30' width='30' height='28' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='105' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>P2</text><rect x='120' y='30' width='50' height='28' fill='#FEF3C7' stroke='#F59E0B'/><text x='145' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>K2</text><rect x='170' y='30' width='30' height='28' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='185' y='49' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>P3</text><text x='10' y='85' fill='#555'>m pointers 8B + m-1 keys 12B <= 1024</text><text x='10' y='102' fill='#555'>20m - 12 <= 1024 => m <= 51.8 => m_max = 51</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Internal node with m pointers and m-1 keys: total bytes = m*8 + (m-1)*12 must be <= 1024, giving m_max = 51.

given.aim: Find maximum m such that m block pointers and (m-1) search keys fit in one block

terms.meaning: 12 bytes per search key stored in an internal node.
terms.connects: Contributes to total node size that must fit in 1024 bytes.

terms.meaning: 8 bytes per pointer to a child block in the tree.
terms.connects: Together with key bytes determines feasibility: 408 + 600 = 1008 <= 1024.

given.plan: Set up inequality m*8 + (m-1)*12 <= 1024, solve for maximum integer m.

steps.title: Set up the node size inequality
steps.note: An internal node has m child pointers and m-1 separator keys; record pointer (10B) is used only in leaf nodes.

steps.title: Choose maximum integer m and verify
steps.note: Always verify both m and m+1 to confirm the boundary.

questions.formula_note: Maximum B+ tree order m is found from the node-capacity constraint: m * ptr_size + (m-1) * key_size <= block_size.

questions.question:
Consider a B+ tree with fan-out equal to 3. You are given an empty B+ tree where nodes can hold up to 5 key values. Values 1, 2, ..., 10 are inserted in order.
(a) Show the tree pictorially after all 5 insertions and after all 10 insertions. Do NOT show intermediate stages.
(b) Suppose instead of splitting a node when it is full, we try to move a value to the left sibling. If no left sibling exists or it is full, split the node. Show the tree after inserting 1, 2, ..., 10 in order.
(c) In general, suppose a B+ tree node can hold a maximum of m keys. Assuming the average number of keys in each leaf-level node is about m/2, what is the average number of keys per leaf node?
A. m/2 + 1
B. (m+1)/2
C. m/2
D. 2m
questions.to_find: The average number of keys in each leaf-level node of a B+ tree when nodes fill to about half capacity on average

understand.plain: When keys are inserted into a B+ tree using standard splitting, each split creates two nodes that are each about half full. Over many insertions the average occupancy converges to ceil(m/2). The formula ceil(m/2) equals (m+1)/2 using integer arithmetic, and this is also the standard minimum leaf occupancy for B+ trees.

keywords.explain: The number of keys stored in a leaf node; ranges from ceil(m/2) to m.
keywords.example: For m=5, min leaf occupancy = ceil(5/2) = 3, max = 5.

keywords.explain: When a full leaf (m keys) gets a new key, it splits into two nodes each with about m/2 keys.
keywords.example: A full node with 3 keys splits into two nodes of about 1-2 keys when the 4th key is inserted.

keywords.explain: Ceiling of m/2; equals (m+1)/2 using integer arithmetic. Represents both minimum and average leaf occupancy.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 380 140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' font-family='system-ui,sans-serif' font-size='12'><rect width='380' height='140' fill='var(--color-bg,#fff)'/><text x='10' y='18' font-weight='bold' fill='var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A)'>B+ tree keys 1..5 max keys m=3 per node</text><text x='10' y='38' fill='#666'>Root internal node</text><rect x='120' y='28' width='60' height='22' rx='3' fill='#FEF3C7' stroke='#F59E0B'/><text x='150' y='43' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>3 | 5</text><line x1='130' y1='50' x2='60' y2='68' stroke='#999'/><line x1='150' y1='50' x2='150' y2='68' stroke='#999'/><line x1='170' y1='50' x2='240' y2='68' stroke='#999'/><rect x='20' y='68' width='70' height='22' rx='3' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='55' y='83' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>1 | 2</text><rect x='115' y='68' width='70' height='22' rx='3' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='150' y='83' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>3 | 4</text><rect x='210' y='68' width='50' height='22' rx='3' fill='#DBEAFE' stroke='#3B82F6'/><text x='235' y='83' text-anchor='middle' fill='#1E293B'>5</text><text x='10' y='115' fill='#555'>Average keys per leaf = ceil(m/2) = (m+1)/2</text><text x='10' y='132' fill='#555'>For m=3: (3+1)/2 = 2 keys on average per leaf.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: After inserting 1..5 into a B+ tree with max 3 keys, leaves hold about ceil(m/2) = 2 keys each on average.

given.aim: Determine the average number of keys per leaf node in a B+ tree where nodes fill to about half capacity

terms.meaning: The upper limit of keys a single leaf node can hold before splitting is triggered.
terms.example: m=3: a node can hold 1, 2, or 3 keys; split triggered when inserting a 4th.
terms.connects: Defines both the split threshold and the minimum occupancy formula.

terms.meaning: The typical number of keys per leaf after many insertions; approximated by ceil(m/2) = (m+1)/2 due to splitting.
terms.example: For m=5: leaves average (5+1)/2 = 3 keys, consistent with min=3, max=5.
terms.connects: This is the answer to the question.

given.plan: The average after splitting is approximately m/2. The exact integer formula is ceil(m/2) = (m+1)/2. Match to the given options.

steps.title: Analyze splitting behavior
steps.apply: When a leaf with m keys splits, the m+1 keys are distributed: ceil((m+1)/2) to one node and floor((m+1)/2) to the other. Each resulting node has about m/2 keys. As insertions fill nodes back to m before the next split, the long-run average converges to ceil(m/2).
steps.note: The average converges to about half of m because each split creates two half-full nodes.

steps.title: Match to answer choices
steps.apply: For m=3: (3+1)/2=2. For m=5: (5+1)/2=3. Option B gives these values. Option A (m/2+1) is too large. Option C (m/2) is non-integer for odd m. Option D (2m) exceeds max capacity. Only option B matches.
steps.note: Option B (m+1)/2 gives the integer ceiling of m/2, matching the minimum occupancy and convergent average.

questions.formula_note: The average number of keys per leaf in a B+ tree after sequential insertions converges to ceil(m/2) = (m+1)/2, equal to the minimum leaf occupancy imposed by the B+ tree half-full constraint.
